description: Trigger discrimination: ONE query's plan verdict (this skill) vs chatty many-query patterns (n-plus-one-detector), unattributed slowness (profiling-methodology-designer), workload placement (operational-vs-analytical-splitter), and structural tenant indexing/partitioning (multi-tenant-data-architect).

cases.prompt: One search query is timing out since the data grew. I've got its EXPLAIN ANALYZE — find the dominant cost and tell me the cheapest real fix.
cases.reason: One named query with plan output in hand is the narrowest tool's home case: dominant node, divergence check, ranked priced fixes. No query-count pattern, no unattributed symptom, no placement question.

cases.prompt: The order list endpoint issues one query per order to fetch its customer — 300 orders, 300 extra queries. Design the fix.
cases.note: SEAM CASE — the sibling boundary: every individual query here is healthy; the defect is the per-row access PATTERN. Plan-reading one of the 300 would find nothing; batching/eager-loading design is the fix.
cases.reason: Query-count explosion at the data-access layer, not a single plan's cost structure.

cases.prompt: The API got slower across the board this quarter — could be the database, could be the app tier, nobody has evidence. Figure out how we find where the time goes.
cases.reason: No query is named and no attribution exists — designing the investigation (attribution level, conditions, narrowing loop) precedes plan-reading. The plan reader starts where a query has been evidenced.

cases.prompt: Even after tuning, the month-end analytics aggregation fundamentally doesn't fit the transactional database — decide whether that workload should move and to what.
cases.note: SEAM CASE — the tuning-headroom boundary: 'after tuning' concedes the plan-level work is done and an order-of-magnitude gap remains. Placement (replica/CDC/materialization) is the splitter's verdict; plan-reading has no 100x to give.
cases.reason: The question is workload placement, not a plan's dominant node.

cases.prompt: Design our indexing and partitioning strategy by tenant across the core tables — hot tenants, shared reference data, the works.
cases.reason: Store-wide tenant indexing/partitioning is structural data-layer design. The plan reader reads how ONE query interacts with existing structures — including tenant predicates — but never designs the tenant strategy itself.
